tweets.full_text:
うつで休職した同僚がいた。

3ヶ月ぶりに復帰した日、
課長が、
「お帰り。まあ、無理しないでね。」

『ありがとうございます。』

「正直に言うと、
　休んでた間、みんな大変だったんだよ。
　それだけは知っといてほしくて。」

同僚は、「すみませんでした」と言った。

復帰初日に、謝った。→

AIっぽさから脱出したい？
プロンプトである程度はましになります。

---
あなたはX運用のプロライターです。
・1行目で結論
・敬語7割、タメ口3割
・「いかがでしたか」「ぜひ」「〜していきましょう」禁止
・体験を1つ混ぜる
---

ただこれだけだと不十分
本気でやるならこれ↓

原則・口調・構文をGPTsやclaude projectにする

これをすると一気に出てくる文章が変わります。

でももっとよくする方法があって・・・・

Most people think Claude Code only works one way.

That’s the mistake.

Free Claude Code Stack:

→ Use the open-source Free Claude Code CLI

→ Point it at free OpenRouter models like Our Alpha

→ Get a 1M token context window for agentic work

→ Run a Claude Code-style workflow without paying Anthropic API fees

Why This Gets Powerful:

✓ Plug it into an Agent Operating System

✓ Preview HTML, images, PDFs, audio, and video inside one workspace

✓ Keep persistent chat history across projects

✓ Scope every chat to its own project folder

✓ Connect Obsidian so your agents stop starting from zero

The CLI alone is useful.

But CLI + memory + workspace preview + multi-agent command center is a different category.

Free coding agents are not the real unlock.

Free coding agents with memory are.

tweets.full_text:
When the Claude Code repo leaked, I sat down with Claude and went through it together. Claude found a skill named skillify in the source, and we reproduced it from the source code.

I've been using it regularly ever since.

It's a skill that turns the session you just finished into a reusable skill.
You do the real work and at the end you type /skillify.
It reads the session JSONL you just generated and walks you through writing the SKILL.md.

The interesting part is how it works. The naive shape is a one-shot generator: read the transcript, infer the steps, write the file.
Plausible output, almost always wrong.
The things that make a skill fire correctly next time — the trigger phrases, the success criteria for each step, whether step 3 needs human confirmation, whether the whole thing should run inline or forked into its own context — don't live in the transcript.

What is unique about it:
Skillify is an interview, not a generator.
It starts with "I already know what you just did, now let's nail it down.":
The input is the session JSONL — your messages, the tool calls, the places you corrected course — and every question gets routed through AskUserQuestion so you click instead of type.
That forces the workflow into a sequence of decisions, not a freeform conversation, and it makes the floor of output quality high even for a lazy user.

Four rounds:
Round 1 confirms the name, description, and goal.
Round 2 lays out the steps and asks about arguments, execution mode, save location.
Round 3 goes step by step, asking what each step produces, what proves it succeeded, whether to pause for human confirmation, what the hard rules are.
Round 4 nails the trigger phrases.

Round 3 is where most of the value is.
The required field is "Success criteria" — every step has to declare what proves it's done.
"Run the tests" is a step.
"Tests pass green, zero skipped" is a success criterion.
The first leaves Claude room to declare victory on a flaky pass. The second forces an artifact.

The output is a SKILL.md with proper frontmatter,
numbered steps with a Success criteria line on each,
and optional annotations for execution mode, artifacts, human checkpoints, and hard rules.
The allowed-tools list is derived from the tool calls Claude actually made in the session you're skillifying — so the permissions match the workflow.

Memory captures facts. Skills capture workflows. Hooks capture enforcement.

So what does skillify actually give you that inline-Claude-writing doesn't?
Contract completeness.
Specifically: the Success criteria field is required on every step.
Without skillify, nothing stops inline-Claude from producing a SKILL.md whose steps say "Run the tests" with no acceptance criterion.
The runtime would then declare victory on whatever plausible-looking output came back.
skillify won't let that file leave the interview — it enforces the criterion field structurally.

Same principle as TDD. skillify makes you write the test.

To try - link in reply.

How To Master Claude in 10 Days

The real power starts when you use:

• Projects for long-term context
• Memory for personalization
• Artifacts for building actual assets
• Claude Code for real development work
• Connectors + MCP for workflow automation
• Skills for reusable systems

Found it helpful? Like, Repost, Bookmark and comment!
.
.
.

tweets.full_text:
There’s an abundance of bad advice out there on AI.

We tested 10+ folder structures across a 20-person AI-native services team.

Here's the optimal Claude Code repo layout we landed on:

1) .claude/skills/
↳ One file per skill, flat in the folder
↳ Each skill handles one task end-to-end

2) .claude/agents/
↳ For longer workflows nested inside a skill

3) .claude/commands/
↳ Slash commands that string multiple skills together

4) .claude/hooks/
↳ Controls what Claude can and can't do based on who's running it
↳ Tiered approvals by user role

5) .claude/rules/
↳ Tells Claude how to behave depending on what file it's working in

The repo root carries CLAUDE(.)md, INDEX(.)md, wiki/, clients/, raw-context/, archive/.

For non-technical GTM teams, this is the #1 reason you NEED to switch to Claude Code.
